perturbation.original_text: the Agent will at its sole expense: (a) meet the Agent's performance criteria set forth in the Business Plan; (b) use its reasonable best efforts to market and promote the sale of the Products to Customers throughout those portions of the Territory in which the Agent has field sales representatives on and after the Agent Launch Date, through direct sales calls, advertising and other appropriate means (all such marketing and promotional activities to be in accordance with the Business Plan or as otherwise expressly proposed by the Agent and approved in writing by the Company);
perturbation.changed_text: the Agent will at its sole expense: (a) endeavor to generally meet the Agent's performance criteria set forth in the Business Plan; (b) use its best judgment to market and promote the sale of the Products to Customers throughout the Territory through means deemed appropriate by the Agent; marketing and promotional activities should generally align with the Business Plan or proposals approved by the Company;
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity by changing 'meet the Agent's performance criteria' to 'endeavor to generally meet,' weakening the obligation. Replacing 'reasonable best efforts' with 'best judgment' and 'appropriate means' makes marketing efforts subjective, not necessarily aligned with the Business Plan. Changing 'in accordance with' to 'generally align with' allows deviations from the Business Plan, reducing its enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to perform contractual obligations
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires good faith and reasonableness in contractual performance. The changes introduce ambiguity that allows the agent to perform with less diligence than originally agreed, potentially undermining the principle of good faith and failing to meet the reasonable expectations established by the contract and business plan, thus contradicting the spirit and potentially the letter of contract law regarding performance standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-306 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-306 Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings Section 2-306 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-306: Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings Section 2–306. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... Section 2-306. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the obligation to meet performance criteria and promotes subjective marketing efforts, conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and reasonable efforts. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, specifically Section 2-306(2) of the Massachusetts General Laws, state that 'a lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale.' The original contract used 'reasonable best efforts,' which aligns with this legal standard. The change to 'best judgment' and allowing deviations from the Business Plan reduces the enforceability and contradicts the requirement to use 'best efforts' to promote sales, as interpreted under Massachusetts law. This introduces ambiguity that allows for less diligence, which undermines the original intent and potentially the letter of the contract law regarding performance standards.

perturbation.original_text: (j) maintain sales and promotion of the Company's Products so as to meet or exceed the previous years annual sales within the same competitive environment.
perturbation.changed_text: (j) aim to generally maintain sales and promotion of the Company's Products, considering the previous years annual sales within the same competitive environment.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'maintain sales and promotion... to meet or exceed' to 'aim to generally maintain sales' weakens the obligation, making it a goal rather than a requirement. Removing the 'or exceed' part lowers the expectations in regards to the amount of sales.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to achieve agreed-upon sales targets
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the obligation to 'aim to generally maintain sales' instead of ensuring sales 'meet or exceed' previous years' figures, the modification undermines the definiteness of the contract's requirements, potentially conflicting with standards for calculating damages related to failure to meet sales expectations. This is relevant under Massachusetts law, which allows for recovery of damages based on lost profits. The ambiguity makes it harder to enforce a claim for lost profits because the expected level of sales is no longer clearly defined.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 106, Article2, Section 2-708 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 106 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE Article 2 SALES Section 2-708 Seller's Damages for Non-acceptance or Repudiation Section 2-708 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2-708: Seller's Damages for Non-acceptance or Repudiation Section 2–708. (1) Subject to subsection (2) and to the provisions of this Article with respect to proof of market price (section 2–723), the measure of damages for non-acceptance or repudiation by the buyer is the difference between the market price at the time and place for tender and the unpaid contract price together with any incidental damages provided in this Article (section 2–710), but less expenses saved in consequence of the buyer's breach. (2) If the measure of damages provided in subsection (1) is inadequate to put the seller in as good a position as performance would have done then the measure of damages is the profit (including reasonable overhead) which the seller would have made from full performance by the buyer, together with any incidental damages provided in this Article (section 2–710), due allowance for costs reasonably incurred and due credit for payments or proceeds of resale. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the sales to 'meet or exceed' the previous years, establishing a clear, enforceable obligation. The changed text weakens this to 'aim to generally maintain sales,' creating an ambiguous goal rather than a concrete requirement. The 'Law Explanation' correctly states that this change undermines the definiteness required for calculating damages related to failure to meet sales expectations, particularly concerning lost profits under Massachusetts law. The scraped law snippet from 'Section 2-708' relates to 'Seller's Damages for Non-acceptance or Repudiation' and discusses the measure of damages, including lost profits, which aligns with the explanation that the changed text makes it harder to enforce such claims because the sales expectation is unclear.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will supply a list confirming such order shipments to the Agent by the end of the next business day.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will, when feasible, supply a list confirming such order shipments to the Agent by the end of the next business day.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "when feasible" introduces a significant degree of ambiguity. The original clause creates a clear obligation to provide a shipment list promptly. The modified version allows the company to delay or omit the list if it deems it infeasible, which creates a loophole.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. While seemingly minor, systematically failing to provide shipment lists 'when feasible' could be deemed an unfair or deceptive practice. The Agent relies on this information for inventory management and sales tracking. By making this obligation conditional and vague, it could be argued that the Company is engaging in a practice that unfairly disadvantages the Agent, potentially violating Chapter 93A.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The added phrase "when feasible" introduces ambiguity that weakens the obligation to provide shipment lists, potentially violating Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2(a), which declares "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful." By making the provision of shipment lists conditional, the modified text allows the company to potentially disadvantage the agent, undermining fair practice and introducing a loophole.
